revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kokugaryō]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、平安時代中期頃から、[[荘園]]に対して公領を指して呼ばれた言葉。国衙は国の役所の意味。
律令制では公地公民が建前であったが、[[墾田永年私財法]]等を経て次第に荘園が多く見られるようになり、律令制は乱れた。その中にあって、国司の勢力下にある公領は、国司の私有財産のように扱われ、国衙領と呼ばれた。国司には任期があるため、荘園とは異なる。<!--在庁官人--->
[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に守護・地頭の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていったが、室町時代にも残っていた。
{{stub}}

revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kokugaryō]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: 発生の経緯を国司請負の成立などとからめて改稿＋α。
revision.text:
'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃から、[[荘園]]に対して公領を指して呼ばれた用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
[[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、[[国司]]へ租税納入を請け負わせる'''国司請負'''へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、国司の勢力下にある公領は、国司の私有財産のように扱われ、国衙領と呼ばれた。このとき、荘園と国衙領（公領）に大きく二分される土地・民衆支配の形態が成立し、これを[[荘園公領制]]と呼ぶ。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていったが、[[室町時代]]にも残存し、[[太閤検地]]により解体された。
[[category:日本史|こくがりょう]] {{History-stub}}

revision.comment: 成立過程を記述
revision.text:
'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の公領を、[[荘園]]に対して呼ぶ[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
[[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせる'''国司請負'''へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。受領は租税納入先の大寺社や摂関家のような上級貴族へ滞納が生じると、勤務評定である受領功過定（ずりょうこうかさだめ）を切り抜けるために、公領の一部の租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与えることが慣例になっていった。こうしてなし崩し的に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（一国平均役）をするため、たびたび荘園整理令が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。こうした体制下の公領を、受領の指揮下にある国衙の領地とみなして、国衙領と呼ぶ。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という呼称を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていったが、[[室町時代]]にも残存し、[[太閤検地]]により解体された。
[[category:日本史|こくがりょう]] {{History-stub}}

国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていったが、[[室町時代]]にも残存し、[[太閤検地]]により解体された。
[[category:日本史|こくがりょう]] {{History-stub}}

== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせる[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。受領は租税納入先の大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ滞納が生じると、勤務評定である受領功過定（ずりょうこうかさだめ）を切り抜けるために、公領の一部の租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与えることが慣例になっていった。こうしてなし崩し的に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。こうした体制下の公領を、受領の指揮下にある国衙の領地とみなして、国衙領と呼ぶ。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていったが、[[室町時代]]にも残存し、[[太閤検地]]により解体された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]]
{{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]に対して呼ぶ[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、現地における実務を「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。受領は租税納入先の大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ滞納が生じると、勤務評定である[[受領功過定]]（ずりょうこうかさだめ）を切り抜けるために、公領の一部の租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与えることが慣例になっていった。こうして徐々に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しく、[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの実際に支配できた土地は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。最終的には[[太閤検地]]により国衙領は廃止された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]] * [[官衙領]]
{{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]に対して呼ぶ[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、現地における実務を「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。受領は租税納入先の大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ滞納が生じると、勤務評定である[[受領功過定]]（ずりょうこうかさだめ）を切り抜けるために、公領の一部の租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与えることが慣例になっていった。こうして徐々に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]] * [[官衙領]]
{{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

revision.text:
'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]に対して呼ぶ[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、現地における実務を「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。受領は租税納入先の大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ滞納が生じると、勤務評定である[[受領功過定]]（ずりょうこうかさだめ）を切り抜けるために、公領の一部の租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与えることが慣例になっていった。こうして徐々に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]] * [[官衙領]] * [[国領駅]] {{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]に対して呼ぶ[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、現地における実務を「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行し始めた。これに伴い、受領の勢力下にある公領は、受領の私有財産のように扱われ始めた。
受領の務めは、国内の公領から徴収した租税を、大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ任期中に納入することで、この実績が勤務評定（[[受領功過定]]）となった。しかし徴収が捗らず滞納が生じると、代わりに、相当する租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与え、直接、徴税を委ねて（国免荘を指定）、切り抜けることが慣例になっていった。こうして徐々に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]] * [[官衙領]] * [[国領駅]] {{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を指す[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
これに対比するものは、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]（＝[[私領]]）。両者を合わせて[[荘園公領制]]と呼ぶことがある。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、朝廷は現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、公領への支配権を大きく移譲した。現地における実務は「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行した。
受領の務めは、国内の公領から徴収した租税を、朝廷および大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ任期中に納入することで、この実績が勤務評定（[[受領功過定]]）となった。しかし徴収が捗らず滞納が生じると、代わりに、相当する租税収納権を納入先の大寺社や摂関家に与え、直接、徴税を委ねて（国免荘を指定）、切り抜けることが慣例になっていった。こうして徐々に公領に虫食い状に発生した新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は、次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税（令制国に対する[[国宛]]で公領に課税される）が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。

これに対比するものは、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]（＝[[私領]]）。両者を合わせて[[荘園公領制]]と呼ぶことがある。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、朝廷は現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、公領への支配権を大きく移譲した。現地における実務は「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行した。

特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税（令制国に対する[[国宛]]で公領に課税される）が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。

revision.text:
'''Kokugaryō''' (国衙領) refers to the [[public land]] (公領) under the control of the [[provincial government office]] (国衙) during the [[Heian period]] and later. The term derives from the [[kokuga]], which was the administrative headquarters of a [[Ritsuryō system]] province.
This term contrasts with [[shōen]] (荘園), or private estates. Together, public land and private estates form the system known as the [[shōen-kōryōsei]] (荘園公領制).
== Overview == From the [[10th century]], the tax system based on [[ancient Japanese household registers|household registers]] and [[Handen-Shūju system]] under the [[Ritsuryō]] code began to collapse. The [[imperial court]] shifted tax collection responsibilities to the chief provincial governors, known as [[zuryō]], transferring significant control over public lands (kokugaryō) to them. The zuryō, in turn, delegated local administrative duties to [[zaichō kanjin]], a class of local elites known as "wealthy notables" (富豪の輩), under the [[kokushi subcontracting system]].
The zuryō's primary duty was to collect taxes from public lands and remit them to the court and powerful entities such as large temples, shrines, or noble families like the [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]. Their performance was evaluated based on their tax remittance records ([[jiryō koka sadame]]). However, when tax collection fell short, zuryō would often cede taxation rights over portions of public land to these entities, resulting in the growth of tax-exempt estates known as [[kokumen shō]] (国免荘). This practice gradually eroded the public land's tax base.
During the [[11th century]], frequent fires in the [[Imperial Palace]] and major temples necessitated reconstruction funds, leading to the imposition of temporary taxes ([[kokuan]]) on public lands. However, the expansion of tax-exempt shōen complicated tax collection. To address this, successive [[shōen consolidation ordinances]] were issued to halt the creation of new shōen after specific benchmark years while formally recognizing pre-existing kokumen shō as taxable lands. These ordinances facilitated the integration of scattered shōen plots into unified territories, simplifying tax collection through regional governance by estate officials.
This marked the transition from a unified provincial administration under zuryō to a bifurcated system where zuryō governed public lands, and estate officials governed shōen, dividing land and population control. The historian [[Amino Yoshihiko]] highlighted this dual structure as central to the concept of the [[shōen-kōryōsei]].
With the establishment of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], kokugaryō increasingly came under the influence of [[shugo]] and [[jitō]]. During the [[Nanboku-chō period]], many kokugaryō were designated as [[hyōrōryōsho]] (provision lands for military supplies), leading to their gradual appropriation as shugo territories. The [[Meitoku Accord]] nominally assigned kokugaryō to the [[Daikakuji line]], but the collection of tributes was minimal, and the line struggled financially. Despite this, kokugaryō persisted even after the [[Ashikaga Yoshinori]] administration forced Daikakuji descendants into monastic life and confiscated their estates. The kokugaryō system was ultimately abolished during the [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] land survey ([[Taikō Kenchi]]).
== Related Articles == * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Shōen-kōryōsei]] * [[Kokushi subcontracting system]] * [[Kangaryō]] (官衙領) * [[Kokuryō Station]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kokugaryō}} [[Category:Japanese estate system]] [[ja:国衙領]]

This marked the transition from a unified provincial administration under zuryō to a bifurcated system where zuryō governed public lands, and estate officials governed shōen, dividing land and population control. The historian [[Amino Yoshihiko]] highlighted this dual structure as central to the concept of the [[shōen-kōryōsei]].
With the establishment of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], kokugaryō increasingly came under the influence of [[shugo]] and [[jitō]]. During the [[Nanboku-chō period]], many kokugaryō were designated as [[hyōrōryōsho]] (provision lands for military supplies), leading to their gradual appropriation as shugo territories. The [[Meitoku Accord]] nominally assigned kokugaryō to the [[Daikakuji line]], but the collection of tributes was minimal, and the line struggled financially. Despite this, kokugaryō persisted even after the [[Ashikaga Yoshinori]] administration forced Daikakuji descendants into monastic life and confiscated their estates. The kokugaryō system was ultimately abolished during the [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] land survey ([[Taikō Kenchi]]).

This term contrasts with [[shōen]] (荘園), or private estates. Together, public land and private estates form the system known as the [[shōen-kōryōsei]] (荘園公領制).
== Overview == From the [[10th century]], the tax system based on [[ancient Japanese household registers|household registers]] and [[Handen-Shūju system]] under the [[Ritsuryō]] code began to collapse. The [[imperial court]] shifted tax collection responsibilities to the chief provincial governors, known as [[zuryō]], transferring significant control over public lands (kokugaryō) to them. The zuryō, in turn, delegated local administrative duties to [[zaichō kanjin]], a class of local elites known as "wealthy notables" (富豪の輩), under the [[kokushi subcontracting system]].
The zuryō's primary duty was to collect taxes from public lands and remit them to the court and powerful entities such as large temples, shrines, or noble families like the [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]. During the [[11th century]], frequent fires in the [[Imperial Palace]] and major temples necessitated reconstruction funds, leading to the imposition of temporary taxes ([[kokuan]]) on public lands. However, the expansion of tax-exempt shōen complicated tax collection. To address this, successive [[shōen consolidation ordinances]] were issued to halt the creation of new shōen after specific benchmark years while formally recognizing pre-existing kokumen shō as taxable lands. These ordinances facilitated the integration of scattered shōen plots into unified territories, simplifying tax collection through regional governance by estate officials.

The historian [[Amino Yoshihiko]] highlighted this dual structure as central to the concept of the [[shōen-kōryōsei]].
With the establishment of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], kokugaryō increasingly came under the influence of [[shugo]] and [[jitō]]. Despite this, kokugaryō persisted even after the [[Ashikaga Yoshinori]] administration forced Daikakuji descendants into monastic life and confiscated their estates. '''Kokugaryō''' (国衙領) refers to the [[public land]] (公領) under the control of the [[provincial government office]] (国衙) during the [[Heian period]] and later. The term derives from the [[kokuga]], which was the administrative headquarters of a [[Ritsuryō system]] province.
This term contrasts with [[shōen]] (荘園), or private estates. Together, public land and private estates form the system known as the [[shōen-kōryōsei]] (荘園公領制).
== Overview == From the [[10th century]], the tax system based on [[ancient Japanese household registers|household registers]] and [[Handen-Shūju system]] under the [[Ritsuryō]] code began to collapse. The [[imperial court]] shifted tax collection responsibilities to the chief provincial governors, known as [[zuryō]], transferring significant control over public lands (kokugaryō) to them. The zuryō, in turn, delegated local administrative duties to [[zaichō kanjin]], a class of local elites known as "wealthy notables" (富豪の輩), under the [[kokushi subcontracting system]].
The zuryō's primary duty was to collect taxes from public lands and remit them to the court and powerful entities such as large temples, shrines, or noble families like the [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]. Their performance was evaluated based on their tax remittance records ([[jiryō koka sadame]]). However, when tax collection fell short, zuryō would often cede taxation rights over portions of public land to these entities, resulting in the growth of tax-exempt estates known as [[kokumen shō]] (国免荘). This practice gradually eroded the public land's tax base.
During the [[11th century]], frequent fires in the [[Imperial Palace]] and major temples necessitated reconstruction funds, leading to the imposition of temporary taxes ([[kokuan]]) on public lands. However, the expansion of tax-exempt shōen complicated tax collection. To address this, successive [[shōen consolidation ordinances]] were issued to halt the creation of new shōen after specific benchmark years while formally recognizing pre-existing kokumen shō as taxable lands. These ordinances facilitated the integration of scattered shōen plots into unified territories, simplifying tax collection through regional governance by estate officials.
This marked the transition from a unified provincial administration under zuryō to a bifurcated system where zuryō governed public lands, and estate officials governed shōen, dividing land and population control. The historian [[Amino Yoshihiko]] highlighted this dual structure as central to the concept of the [[shōen-kōryōsei]].
With the establishment of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], kokugaryō increasingly came under the influence of [[shugo]] and [[jitō]]. During the [[Nanboku-chō period]], many kokugaryō were designated as [[hyōrōryōsho]] (provision lands for military supplies), leading to their gradual appropriation as shugo territories. The [[Meitoku Accord]] nominally assigned kokugaryō to the [[Daikakuji line]], but the collection of tributes was minimal, and the line struggled financially. Despite this, kokugaryō persisted even after the [[Ashikaga Yoshinori]] administration forced Daikakuji descendants into monastic life and confiscated their estates. The kokugaryō system was ultimately abolished during the [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] land survey ([[Taikō Kenchi]]).
== Related Articles == * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Shōen-kōryōsei]] * [[Kokushi subcontracting system]] * [[Kangaryō]] (官衙領) * [[Kokuryō Station]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Original pages]] to [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kokugaryō''' (国衙領) refers to the [[public land]] (公領) under the control of the [[provincial government office]] (国衙) during the [[Heian period]] and later. The term derives from the [[kokuga]], which was the administrative headquarters of a [[Ritsuryō system]] province.
This term contrasts with [[shōen]] (荘園), or private estates. Together, public land and private estates form the system known as the [[shōen-kōryōsei]] (荘園公領制).
== Overview == From the [[10th century]], the tax system based on [[ancient Japanese household registers|household registers]] and [[Handen-Shūju system]] under the [[Ritsuryō]] code began to collapse. The [[imperial court]] shifted tax collection responsibilities to the chief provincial governors, known as [[zuryō]], transferring significant control over public lands (kokugaryō) to them. The zuryō, in turn, delegated local administrative duties to [[zaichō kanjin]], a class of local elites known as "wealthy notables" (富豪の輩), under the [[kokushi subcontracting system]].
The zuryō's primary duty was to collect taxes from public lands and remit them to the court and powerful entities such as large temples, shrines, or noble families like the [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]. Their performance was evaluated based on their tax remittance records ([[jiryō koka sadame]]). However, when tax collection fell short, zuryō would often cede taxation rights over portions of public land to these entities, resulting in the growth of tax-exempt estates known as [[kokumen shō]] (国免荘). This practice gradually eroded the public land's tax base.
During the [[11th century]], frequent fires in the [[Imperial Palace]] and major temples necessitated reconstruction funds, leading to the imposition of temporary taxes ([[kokuan]]) on public lands. However, the expansion of tax-exempt shōen complicated tax collection. To address this, successive [[shōen consolidation ordinances]] were issued to halt the creation of new shōen after specific benchmark years while formally recognizing pre-existing kokumen shō as taxable lands. These ordinances facilitated the integration of scattered shōen plots into unified territories, simplifying tax collection through regional governance by estate officials.
This marked the transition from a unified provincial administration under zuryō to a bifurcated system where zuryō governed public lands, and estate officials governed shōen, dividing land and population control. The historian [[Amino Yoshihiko]] highlighted this dual structure as central to the concept of the [[shōen-kōryōsei]].
With the establishment of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], kokugaryō increasingly came under the influence of [[shugo]] and [[jitō]]. During the [[Nanboku-chō period]], many kokugaryō were designated as [[hyōrōryōsho]] (provision lands for military supplies), leading to their gradual appropriation as shugo territories. The [[Meitoku Accord]] nominally assigned kokugaryō to the [[Daikakuji line]], but the collection of tributes was minimal, and the line struggled financially. Despite this, kokugaryō persisted even after the [[Ashikaga Yoshinori]] administration forced Daikakuji descendants into monastic life and confiscated their estates. The kokugaryō system was ultimately abolished during the [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] land survey ([[Taikō Kenchi]]).
== Related Articles == * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Shōen-kōryōsei]] * [[Kokushi subcontracting system]] * [[Kangaryō]] (官衙領) * [[Kokuryō Station]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[historical studies]] referring to the [[public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領}}) from the mid-[[Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|ja|令制国}}.
This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}}.
== Overview == By the [[10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|ja|朝廷}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}}.
The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂関家}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the {{ill|11th century|ja|11世紀}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領}} * {{ill|Kokuryō Station|ja|国領駅}}
{{japanese-history-stub}}

The {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|ja|朝廷}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領}}. The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂関家}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the {{ill|11th century|ja|11世紀}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領}} * {{ill|Kokuryō Station|ja|国領駅}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[historical studies]] referring to the [[public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領}}) from the mid-[[Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|ja|令制国}}.
This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}}.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領}} * {{ill|Kokuryō Station|ja|国領駅}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:kk]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[historical studies]] referring to the [[public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領}}) from the mid-[[Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|ja|令制国}}.
This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}}.
== Overview == By the [[10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|ja|朝廷}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}}.
The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂関家}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the {{ill|11th century|ja|11世紀}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.

However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領}} * {{ill|Kokuryō Station|ja|国領駅}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[historical studies]] referring to the [[public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領}}) from the mid-[[Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|ja|令制国}}.
This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}}.
== Overview == By the [[10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|ja|朝廷}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}}.
The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂関家}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the {{ill|11th century|ja|11世紀}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|ja|網野善彦}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}} * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領}} * {{ill|Kokuryō Station|ja|国領駅}}
{{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kokugaryō}} [[Category:shrine s]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kokugaryō}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[historical studies]] referring to the [[public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領}}) from the mid-[[Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|ja|令制国}}.

revision.text:
'''国衙領'''（こくがりょう）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃以降の[[公領]]を指す[[歴史学]]用語。[[国衙]]は[[令制国|国]]の役所の意味。
これに対比するものは、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]（＝[[私領]]）。両者を合わせて[[荘園公領制]]と呼ぶことがある。
== 概要 == [[10世紀]]頃から、[[律令制]]で規定していた[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍]]・[[班田収授]]による租税制度がほぼ崩壊し、朝廷は現地派遣の筆頭[[国司]]である[[受領]]へ租税納入を請け負わせ、公領への支配権を大きく移譲した。現地における実務は「[[富豪の輩]]」と呼ばれる階層からなる[[在庁官人]]らに委任する[[国司請負制|国司請負]]へと移行した。
受領の務めは、国内の公領から徴収した租税を朝廷および大寺社や[[摂関家]]のような上級貴族へ任期中に納入することであり、この実績が勤務評定（[[受領功過定]]）となった。しかし徴収が捗らず滞納が生じると、代わりに相当する租税収納権を、納入先の大寺社や摂関家に委ねて切り抜けることが通例になっていった。直接徴税の対象となったこれらの土地は公領に虫食い状に生じ拡大した。これらの新立荘園、つまり国免荘（こくめんのしょう）は次第に公領からの租税徴集を圧迫していった。
特に[[11世紀]]には[[内裏]]や大寺社の火災が多く、これらの再建を目的とした臨時課税（令制国に対する[[国宛]]で公領に課税される）が困難になる状況を来した。そのため、単なる既成事実であった国免荘にも課税（[[一国平均役]]）をするため、たびたび[[荘園整理令]]が出された。これらの荘園整理令はある基準年以降の新立荘園を停止する一方、それ以前に発生していた国免荘を臨時課税の対象として正式に公認化するものでもあった。それまでの荘園は上級貴族や大寺社が独占的に租税収納権をもつ農地が公領の農地の中に散在しているに過ぎなかったが、これらの荘園整理令に際し、公領と荘園を識別して一国平均役を課することを容易にするため、荘園を一つの領域に統合する措置が行われた。ここに上級貴族や大寺社が派遣した荘官が、行政や徴税を国家から依託される、統治領域としての荘園が成立した。
こうしてそれまで各国の体制は、荘園も含めて一元的に受領の支配下にあったものが、荘官の支配する荘園と、受領の支配下にある公領が、大きく二分して土地・民衆支配をする形態に移行した。また、[[歴史学者]]の[[網野善彦]]は、中世日本の社会が荘園と公領から構成されていることに注目して[[荘園公領制]]という概念を提唱した。<!--在庁官人--->
国衙領は、[[鎌倉幕府]]が成立すると次第に[[守護]]・[[地頭]]の勢力の下に置かれるようになっていった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の争乱では[[兵粮料所]]に指定された後になし崩しに守護領とされるなどの侵食が激しかった。[[明徳の和約]]では国衙領を[[大覚寺統]]の所領としたものの、実際に納められた年貢は僅かであり、大覚寺統は困窮したという。とはいえ、[[足利義教]]の代に大覚寺統の男子を残らず出家させてその所領が収公された後にも国衙領は存続し、最終的には[[太閤検地]]により廃止された。
== 関連項目 == * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] * [[荘園公領制]] * [[国司請負制]] * [[官衙領]] * [[国領駅]] {{japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:こくかりよう}} [[category:日本の荘園制]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[国衙領]] to [[Kokugaryō]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:国衙領]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11421344]]
Wikidata name: kokugaryō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11421344]]
Wikidata name: kokugaryō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|qq=|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|qq=|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

== Overview ==
By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|qq=|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|qq=|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|qq=|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|qq=|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|qq=|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|qq=|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|qq=|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|qq=|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|qq=|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|qq=|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==
* [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]
* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|qq=|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}
* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|qq=|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

{{japanese-history-stub}}

{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|国衙領|version=101657435|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 1]]

By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|qq=|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|qq=|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|qq=|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|qq=|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|qq=|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|qq=|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|qq=|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|qq=|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|qq=|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|qq=|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|qq=|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). == Overview ==
By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province).
