The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.
== See Also == * [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}} * [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]
{{japanese-history-stub}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:国衙領]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11421344]] Wikidata name: kokugaryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11421344]] Wikidata name: kokugaryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].
This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.
== Overview == By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.
In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.
These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.
As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.
With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.
== See Also == * [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] * {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} * {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}} * [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==
* [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]
* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}
* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* [[:en:Kokuryō Station|Kokuryō Station]]

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]].

This concept is contrasted with [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

== Overview ==
By the [[:en:10th century|10th century]], the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] had largely collapsed. The [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court in Kyoto]] began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the [[:en:11th century|11th century]], frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. [[:en:Yoshihiko Amino|Yoshihiko Amino]], a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], public domains gradually fell under the influence of [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] (provincial constables) and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (manorial stewards). During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|WD=Q7063|lt=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|WD=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}}
* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}
* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* {{ill|Kokuryō Station|en|Kokuryō Station|WD=Q4227494|lt=Kokuryō Station}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan}}.

This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

== Overview ==
By the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century}}, the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|WD=Q7063|lt=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|WD=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}

== Overview ==
By the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century}}, the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|WD=Q7063|lt=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|WD=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}}
* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}
* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Kangaryō (state lands)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* {{ill|Kokuryō Station|en|Kokuryō Station|WD=Q4227494|lt=Kokuryō Station}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan}}.

This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

By the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century}}, the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Kokumen-shō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|WD=Q7063|lt=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Kokuan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Shōen Consolidation Orders|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|WD=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.

Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

== Overview ==
By the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century|1=10th century}}, the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=Koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto|1=Royal court}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Q11420479|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|WD=Q7063|lt=11th century|1=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Q11420477|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Q11618907|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|WD=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino|1=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193888}}.


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}}
* {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}
* {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kangaryō (state lands)|ja|官衙領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangaryō (state lands)|1=Q11452136|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452136}}
* {{ill|Kokuryō Station|en|Kokuryō Station|WD=Q4227494|lt=Kokuryō Station|1=Kokuryō Station}}

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65249135}}) from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan|1=Provinces of Japan}}.

This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618906}}.

'''Kokuga-ryō''' (国衙領) is a term in [[:en:Historical studies|Historical studies]] referring to the [[:en:public domains|public domains]] ({{ill|Kōryō|ja|公領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryō|1=kouryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65249135}}) from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The term "kokuga" refers to the government offices of the {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|qid=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan|1=Provinces of Japan}}.

This concept is contrasted with {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|qid=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}} (private domains). Together, they form the basis of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618906}}.

== Overview ==
By the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|qid=Q8052|lt=10th century|1=10th century}}, the tax system based on {{ill|Ancient Japanese census system|ja|古代の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese census system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=Koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410913}} and {{ill|Handen shūju|ja|班田収授|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen shūju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 班田収授法|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8192257}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} had largely collapsed. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto|1=Royal court}} began delegating tax collection responsibilities to the local {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} (provincial governors), particularly the chief governors known as {{ill|Zuryō|ja|受領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuryō|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410703}}. The zuryō, in turn, entrusted the practical tasks to a class of local officials known as {{ill|Zaichō kanjin|ja|在庁官人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zaichō kanjin|1=zaichō kannin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11423949}}, often from the landowning class referred to as the "wealthy class" (富豪の輩), under a system called {{ill|Kokushi contract system|ja|国司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi contract system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The zuryō's primary role was to ensure the collection and remittance of taxes from public domains to the court and high-Ranking nobles, such as the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|qid=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}} and major temples, during their tenure. When tax collection was delayed, the right to collect the equivalent taxes was often transferred to these entities, creating patches of tax-exempt land known as {{ill|Kokumen-shō|ja|国免荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumen-shō|1=Q11420479|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420479}}, which gradually expanded. These newly established shōen increasingly pressured the collection of taxes from public domains.

In the {{ill|11th century|en|11th century|qid=Q7063|lt=11th century|1=11th century}}, frequent fires at the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|内裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Dairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3012148}} and major temples necessitated extraordinary taxation ({{ill|Kokuan|ja|国宛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国充|1=Q11420477|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420477}}) on public domains to fund reconstruction. However, tax collection became increasingly challenging, and shōen that were previously unofficially exempted from taxation were included under {{ill|Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|ja|一国平均役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikoku Heimin-yaku|1=ikkokuheikinyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11352489}} (an equal distribution of tax burdens across all domains within a province). To formalize this system, the court issued {{ill|Shōen Consolidation Orders|ja|荘園整理令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen Consolidation Orders|1=Q11618907|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618907}}, which stopped the creation of new shōen from a certain year but also officially recognized existing kokumen-shō, subjecting them to temporary taxation.

These orders also simplified taxation by consolidating scattered shōen into single entities, allowing the aristocracy or major temples to send {{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618914}} (estate managers) to administer and collect taxes as delegated by the state. Thus, shōen evolved into governed territories with a unified administrative structure.

As a result, provincial administration shifted from unified control under the zuryō to a dual system of land and population governance, divided between shōen managed by shōkan and public domains controlled by zuryō. {{ill|Yoshihiko Amino|en|Yoshihiko Amino|qid=Q8015317|lt=Yoshihiko Amino|1=Yoshihiko Amino}}, a prominent historian, introduced the concept of the {{ill|Shōen-Kōryō system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen-Kōryō system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618906}} to describe medieval Japanese society composed of these two land systems.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, public domains gradually fell under the influence of {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|qid=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} (provincial constables) and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}} (manorial stewards). During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, public domains were designated as {{ill|Military provisioning areas|ja|兵粮料所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Military provisioning areas|1=hyourouryousho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11393687}} but were often absorbed into shugo territories. Even though the {{ill|Meitoku Agreement|ja|明徳の和約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meitoku Agreement|1=Meitoku Peace Accord|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11087845}} designated public domains as the property of the {{ill|Daikakuji line|ja|大覚寺統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikakuji line|1=Daikakuji line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10937983}}, only minimal taxes were actually collected, leaving the Daikakuji line in financial distress. Despite this, public domains persisted until they were ultimately abolished during the {{ill|Taikō land surveys|ja|太閤検地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō land surveys|1=Taikō kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193888}}.
